next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The phone started its crawling again while Marcia had both hands in the sink.

It jittered somewhere under the couch cushion where Delroy had shoved it, buzzing against the cracked vinyl with the same stubborn nerve a trapped roach had when bleach hit it and did not kill it fast enough. Marcia stood at the basin with Micah's white school polo sunk in gray water, working soap into the brown half-moon under the collar where his blood had dried after the clinic. The cheap lemon detergent had already roughened the skin across her knuckles. Every time the phone shook, the water in the sink gave a faint answer off the pipe.

"Answer it," she said.

Delroy lay lengthwise on the narrow bed in his undershirt, one forearm over his eyes, his right shoulder propped on a pillow that had gone flat months ago. "In a minute."

"You said that two calls ago."

"Then maybe I mean it serious now."

He smiled when he said it, not enough to show teeth, just enough to make it sound like weather instead of warning. That smile had bought him more time in this world than wages ever had. Marcia wrung out the shirt hard enough to hear stitches complain and hung it over the faucet for another soak.

The apartment held everybody's evening at once. Tiana sat at the little table under the window with her document folder open in square anxious piles: originals, copies, things still needed. She had clipped her hair up with a black claw clip and was reading a form so closely Marcia could see her mouth move on the instructions without sound. Micah sprawled on the floor with one sneaker off, one...
open_hooks_to_carry: The trunk functions as family archive, not magic solution.
The document hunt remains concrete and procedural.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...iplinary stamp.

"They still want this back Monday?" she asked.

"Yeah."

"Then they'll get it back Monday."

He watched her read the highlighted lines. Behavior expectations. Reentry conditions. Parent cooperation. Student accountability. All the clean words institutions used when they wanted to describe pressure without naming who got to apply it. She did not make a speech. She only kept smoothing the pages flat, even the one tire had nearly taken, until the paper stopped trying to curl.

At the desk, the receptionist called another name. A woman rose, gathered up her purse and pharmacy bag, and went through the door. The fee sheet under the plexiglass caught the fluorescent light so hard it looked wet.

Micah pressed the gauze tighter. His cheek throbbed. His wrists burned. Behind all of it was the bright fixed image that would not leave: his own face in the silver hood, flattened and red, while people he knew watched from the sidewalk and decided how to carry it home. The officers had uncuffed him and driven off, and the neighborhood had exhaled, and still the day kept charging them for what it had done.

When the nurse finally opened the door and called his name, Marcia stood first. She tucked the forms back into the envelope, slipped the receipt into her wallet, and lifted her tote. Ninety dollars gone before anyone had even touched the wound clean. Micah rose with the gauze pressed to his face and followed her down the bright hallway understanding, with a clarity that made him colder than the air-conditioning, that being let go was not the same thing as being spared.
state_deltas_required: Renew Tiana's documents herself and salvage some version of college.
Tiana's anger stops being simple; she sees both parents as interrupted people rather than only obstacles.
